coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodot-based humidity sensor for self-powered respiratory monitoring
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Jinxu
Yang, Xigui
Shen, Chenglong
Chang, Yu
Deng, Yuan
Zhang, Zhenfeng
Liu, Hang
Lv, Chaofan
Li, Yizhe
Zhang, Chuang
Dong, Lin
Shan, ChongXin
coredata.dc:description: Developing low-cost, portable, and high-performance humidity sensors for respiratory monitoring has received considerable attention in recent years. Herein, humidity sensors have been fabricated based on carbon nanodots (CDs), which exhibit high sensitivity (5318%) at 94% relative humidity (RH) and excellent long-time stability under the RH range of 11% to 94%. The high sensitivity can be attributed to the adsorption of the water molecules by the massive hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of CDs. By introducing a breath-driven triboelectric nanogenerator (TENG), the self-powered humidity sensor is developed for the first time, exhibiting a wide sensing range (11–94% RH) and excellent stability. The maximum output voltage of the TENG is up to 200 V and the maximum short-circuit current is about 9.2 μA. Furthermore, the self-powered humidity sensor further demonstrates the potential ability for real-time respiration monitoring, which can detect different breathing statuses. This work provides a convenient and low-cost strategy for constructing sensitive CDs-based humidity sensors, and prospects their applications in respiratory monitoring systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodot
Humidity sensor
Triboelectric nanogenerator
Self-powered device
Respiratory monitoring

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 12174348 , 52072345 , 62027816 , and 11974317 ), the Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by CAST ( 2021QNRC001 ), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Nos. 2018M630830 and 2019T120631 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province ( 212300410410 ), and Henan Center for Outstanding Overseas Scientists ( GZS201903 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs. a) High-resolution TEM image of the CDs. b) AFM image of the CDs. The inset shows the height profile along the line in b). c) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectrum of the CDs. The inset presents photographs of the CDs aqueous solution under sunlight and 365 nm light. d) XRD pattern, e) FT-IR spectrum and f) XPS full survey of the CDs. g-i) High-resolution XPS C1s, O1s and N1s spectra of the CDs.

Characterization of the CD humidity sensor. a) Schematic diagram of the CD humidity sensor measurement platform. b) I-V characteristics of the humidity sensor at ambient conditions with different RHs. c) I-t characteristics of the CD humidity sensor at ambient conditions with different RHs under 50 V bias. d) The response of the humidity sensor as a function of relative humidity. e) The response of our humidity sensor in comparison with other humidity-sensitive materials. f) Repeatability of the CD humidity sensor from 11% RH to three different (58%, 75% and 94%) RH for four cycles. g) Stability of the humidity sensor under different RHs for 30 days.
Schematic diagram of the humidity sensing mechanism for the CDs humidity sensor. a) low humidity and b) high humidity.

CDs humidity sensor for respiratory monitoring display. a) Schematic illustration of respiratory monitoring of the CDs humidity sensor fixed on a mask. b) Schematic diagram of the adsorption process of water molecules and c) the current changes of the sensor during exhalation. d) Schematic diagram of the desorption process of water molecules and e) the current changes of the sensor during inhalation. f) The response curves of CDs humidity sensor with rapid, normal and deep breathing patterns. The inset shows the enlarged view in the dashed box.

Preparation and characterization of self-powered respiratory monitoring system. a) Schematic illustration of human breathing monitoring with the TENG-CDs humidity sensor system. Open circuit voltage b) and short circuit current c) of TENG. d) The current of the TENG-CDs humidity sensor system at ambient conditions with different RH levels. e) The current of the TENG-CDs humidity sensor system as a function of relative humidity. f) The response curves of different breathing modes, and the enlarged response curves corresponding to g) rapid, h) normal, and i) deep breathing made, respectively.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodot-based humidity sensor for self-powered respiratory monitoring

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Key Laboratory of Diamond Optoelectronic Materials and Devices, Key Laboratory of Materials Physics, Ministry of Education, and School of Physics & Microelectronics, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450052, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of Diamond Optoelectronic Materials and Devices, Key Laboratory of Materials Physics, Ministry of Education, and School of Physics & Microelectronics, Zhengzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Key Laboratory of Diamond Optoelectronic Materials and Devices, Key Laboratory of Materials Physics, Ministry of Education, and School of Physics & Microelectronics, Zhengzhou University, 450052, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing low-cost, portable, and high-performance humidity sensors for respiratory monitoring has received considerable attention in recent years. Herein, humidity sensors have been fabricated based on carbon nanodots (CDs), which exhibit high sensitivity (5318%) at 94% relative humidity (RH) and excellent long-time stability under the RH range of 11% to 94%. The high sensitivity can be attributed to the adsorption of the water molecules by the massive hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of CDs. By introducing a breath-driven triboelectric nanogenerator (TENG), the self-powered humidity sensor is developed for the first time, exhibiting a wide sensing range (11–94% RH) and excellent stability. The maximum output voltage of the TENG is up to 200 V and the maximum short-circuit current is about 9.2 μA. Furthermore, the self-powered humidity sensor further demonstrates the potential ability for real-time respiration monitoring, which can detect different breathing statuses. This work provides a convenient and low-cost strategy for constructing sensitive CDs-based humidity sensors, and prospects their applications in respiratory monitoring systems.

Humidity sensors have been fabricated based on carbon nanodots, which exhibit a high response (5318%) at 94% relative humidity (RH), and excellent long-time stability in the RH range of 11–94%. By introducing the breath-driven triboelectric nanogenerators, self-powered humidity sensors are developed for real-time respiration monitoring to detect variant breathing modes.
ga1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Respiratory monitoring is one of the most important physiological signs for human health care, which can provide vital health information and predict potential disease risks [1,2]. However, the current respiration monitoring devices are restricted due to their high cost, complex structure, poor portability, and reliance on external power sources. Therefore, developing a low-cost, simple structured, and self-powered system for respiratory monitoring is highly desired.

In recent years, many efforts have been focused on the sensors related to the respiratory signals, including pressure [3,4], airflow [5], and humidity [6,7] for real-time respiratory monitoring with the increasing demand for health management. As a result of the relatively high humidity around the nose and mouth during the breathing process, detecting humidity changes during inhalation and exhalation has the potential to conveniently monitor respiration behaviors [8]. Many humidity-sensitive materials such as polymers[9], semiconductors [10–12], and perovskites [13,14] have been investigated. However, most of these sensors either suffer from low sensitivity, possible biological toxicity, or are dependent on batteries or external power sources.

Carbon nanomaterials have been widely used in humidity sensors because of their low cost, nontoxicity, good biocompatibility, and high stability [15–17]. In addition to the above features, carbon nanodots (CDs) have large specific surface areas and abundant hydrophilic functional groups [18–27], which are favorable to capture plenty of water molecules and improve the performance of humidity sensing. Also, a self-powered breathing monitoring system with decent flexibility and stability is promising for daily use. In this regard, triboelectric nanogenerators (TENGs) have emerged as the technology for energy harvesting in recent years, which can convert randomly distributed or irregular mechanical energy into electrical energy, and have broad potential applications in self-powered systems [28–35]. Therefore, TENG-driven CDs humidity sensors make them possible to construct a self-powered and low-cost sensing system for real-time respiratory monitoring.

In this work, we report a low-cost and high-performance humidity sensor based on CDs with high sensitivity, wide sensing range, and excellent repeatability and long-term stability. By integrating it with the TENG, a self-powered humidity sensor system has been developed for real-time respiration monitoring, which can be used to monitor breath conditions.

The CDs were synthesized by a microwave heating method. Citric acid monohydrate (1 g) and urea (2 g) were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water. The mixture was then heated in a microwave oven for 5 min to obtain a dark solid. After being cooled down to room temperature, the product was dissolved in deionized water and then filtered to obtain CDs solution for subsequent use.

Firstly, the polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrate was ultrasonically cleaned in acetone, ethanol and deionized water successively to remove possible organic contaminants. Interdigital Au electrodes of 500 µm in length and 5 µm in width were then obtained by photolithography on the PET substrate. Finally, the CDs aqueous solution was dropped on the interdigital electrodes to form a humidity sensor.

Oxygen plasma-pretreated polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) was used as the negative friction layer. The negative charge collector is obtained by sputtering a layer of copper on the Kapton film. The positive friction layer and corresponding charge collector is a piece of copper foil glued to a 3 M foam tape.

The morphology of the as-synthesized CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEOL-2010) operating at 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded by a D/MAX-3B diffractometer (Rigaku) using Cu Kα radiation. The chemical composition was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron energy spectra (XPS) using a Thermo scientific Escalab 250Xi spectrometer employing Al radiation as the excitation source. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra was measured on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iZ 10 spectrometer in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. Atomic force microscopy (AIST SmartSPM) was used to measure the diameter distribution of the CDs. The absorption spectrum of the CDs was obtained on a Hitachi UH-4150 UV–VIS–NIR spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectrum of the CDs solution was collected on a spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7000). The current-voltage (I-V) and current-time (I-t) characteristics of the device were measured using a semiconductor parameter analyzer (Keithley 4200 s). The output performance of TENG was measured by a Keithley Model 6514 high-impedance electrometer. The sensing responses of the humidity sensors were measured using saturated salt solutions as the humidity source. Different relative humidity levels were obtained by dissolving LiCl (RH 11%), C2H3KO2 (RH 23%), K2CO3 (RH 43%), NaBr (RH 57%), KI (RH 69%), NaCl (RH 75%), KCl (RH 84%), and KNO3 (RH 94%) into deionized water in closed glass containers at 25 °C.

To construct a hydrophilic surface for humidity sensing, citric acid is utilized as the precursor to synthesize CDs. Fig. 1a shows the high-resolution TEM image of the CDs, a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm can be observed, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [19,20,36]. Atomic force microscope (AFM) image (Fig. 1b) reveals an average height of approximately 8 nm. The UV−Vis absorption spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 1c) in solution shows two main strong absorption bands at 360 nm and 420 nm, which can be assigned to the π → π * transition of the core and the n → π * transitions of the surface states, respectively [22,37–39]. Under the optimal excitation of 470 nm, it shows a fluorescent emission at around 560 nm. The aqueous CD solution shows green fluorescence under the excitation of 365 nm UV light. X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) in Fig. 1d exhibits a narrow peak centered at about 26.8º, which is the typical (002) scattering of the graphite structure [38,40], revealing the high degree of crystallinity of the CDs. The chemical component of the CDs is investigated by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The broad IR absorption centered at 3444 cm−1 is assigned to the O-H stretching vibration; while other absorption peaks at 1640, 1401, and 1049 cm−1 correspond to the skeletal vibrations of CO stretching, C-N stretching, and C-O stretching, respectively [41–43]. The XPS full survey evidences the presence of C, O, and N elements (Fig. 1f) with atomic contents of 50.94%, 25.38% and 23.68%, respectively, indicating that the CDs are carbonized with a high O and N level. As shown in Fig. 1e, the high-resolution C1s spectrum reveals four different types of C: CC/C-C (284.8 eV), C-C/C-N (285.4 eV), C(O)-O- (288.7 eV) and CO (289.8 eV) [44,45]. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) could be deconvolved into two peaks at 531.7 and 532.4 eV, which are assigned to C-O and OC-O groups, respectively [46,47]. In the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1g), two peaks at 400.1 and 400.9 eV are attributed to amino and graphitic N, respectively [40,48,49]. The XPS results confirmed that the surface of CDs contains abundant hydrophilic groups, which is consistent with the results of FT-IR analysis.

In order to evaluate the humidity sensing performance, the CD-based humidity sensor was constructed and then placed in various relative humidity (RH) conditions by the saturated salt solution method [50,51]. The schematic illustration of the device structure and the test platform is shown in Fig. 2a. Fig. 2b exhibits the current-voltage (I-V) curves of the device under different RHs. The current raises from 0.2 μA to 10.7 μA with the increase of RH value from 11–94%. This device is further examined through response and recovery cycles under different RHs, as shown in Fig. 2c. This indicates its extraordinary humidity-dependent behavior in the RH range of 11–94%. Response (R) is a significant parameter in a humidity sensor, which is defined as follows [52].
(1)
R
%
=
I
x
−
I
0
I
0
× 100
where I
0 and I
x are the current of the device at 11% and x% RH, respectively. At low RH (less than 57%), the response of humidity sensor changes slightly, but increases obviously at higher RH (Fig. 2d). At 94% RH, the response can reach approximately 5318%, significantly higher than that of other previously reported humidity sensors (Fig. 2e) [15,16,53–58].

To explore the repeatability of the sensor, Fig. 2f illustrates its response from 11% RH to three different (58%, 75% and 94%) RHs for four cycles. It is found that the device exhibits a reproducible absorption and desorption behavior with little fluctuations, implying an excellent repeatability and reliability. The long-term stability of the humidity sensor has been further investigated. Fig. 2g shows the I–t curve of the device in various RH levels for 30 days. It can be seen that the current values of the sensor remain almost constant for each RH level during one month, proving the excellent stability and durability of our sensor. These results demonstrate that CDs humidity sensors have outstanding sensitivity and stability, making them potential candidates for monitoring human respiratory.

Based on the above results, we assumed that the humidity-sensing characteristics of the sensor may depend on the adsorption and desorption of water molecules on the surface of CDs. Like other reported moisture-sensitive materials, water molecules adsorbed on the CDs surfaces increase with the increase of humidity, resulting in the change of the electrical conductivity. There are many studies on how water molecules adsorbed on the surface affect the electrical conductivity of materials. The adsorbed water molecules on the surface of materials can be classified as chemisorption and physical adsorption. The water molecules are adsorbed on the sites by chemisorption in a low humidity environment and undergo a dissociation process and release the proton H+ [59,60]. Small amounts of water molecules are adsorbed on the surface of the material and ion migration is difficult, resulting in poor conductivity at low humidity conditions. Water molecules are not only chemisorbed on the material surface under high humidity conditions, but also form a continuous water layer by physical adsorption on the material surface. The increasing freedom of ions to move in the water layer leads to an increase in the electrical conductivity of the material. As shown in Fig. 3, we propose a model of water molecules adsorbed on the surface of CDs to illustrate its conduction mechanism. At a low humidity, a small amount of water molecules adsorbs on the hydrophilic surface of CDs (Fig. 3a), and the ionic migration is difficult, thus resulting in the poor response in low RH. With the increase of RH, more water molecules are adsorbed on the surface of CDs to form a continuous water layer and the ions will have more and more freedom to move inside the water layers (Fig. 3b) [59]. According to the Grotthuss transfer mechanism [60]:
(2)
H
2
O +
H
3
O
+
=
H
3
O
+
+ H
2
O
the resulting transfer of H3O+ between adjacent hydrones becomes more easier, which leads to a larger response of the CDs humidity sensor at higher relative humidity as shown in Fig. 2d. As a result, this CDs-based humidity sensor has a low current at low humidity (Fig. S1a) and a significantly higher current at high humidity (Fig. S1b).

Motivated by the high performance of the CD-based humidity sensor, its potential application for human breath monitoring was investigated. The humidity sensor is integrated with a commercial mask to detect real-time monitoring of human breathing ( Fig. 4a). During exhalation, a large amount of water molecules will be adsorbed on the CD surface, leading to the current increase of the sensor (Figs. 4b and 4c). In contrast, the adsorbed water molecules during inhalation will desorb, which causes the sensor current to decrease to the original level (Figs. 4d and 4e). Fig. 4f clearly shows the reliable signals of different respiratory rates (deep, normal and rapid breathing), where rapid breathing changes more rapidly than normal and deep breathing, and the deep breathing has a longer breath cycle and higher breath intensity. Compared with normal breathing, deep breathing mode with low frequency generates a series of stronger electrical signals due to more water molecules layers on the sensor. The distinct electric signal patterns thus allow direct recognition of different breath states. These results confirm that the CDs sensor can be used to monitor the moisture in real time during expiration and inspiration process. However, such method to monitor breathing is not portable since it requires an external power supply to the humidity sensor.

Based on the advanced respiration monitoring capability, we further develop a self-powered breathing monitoring system by combining the TENG ( Fig. 5a). The TENG is made of Cu and PTFE as the positive and negative electrode to collect charges, respectively [61,62]. As illustrated in Fig. S2, the CDs humidity sensor is attached on the upper surface of the TENG, endowing the ability of the test system to sense both the kinetic energy and the humidity changes generated during exhalation. The output performance of the simple TENG is investigated and shown in Figs. 5b and 5c. The highest output voltage can reach 200 V, and the maximum short current is about 9.2 μA. Hence, the TENG enables to drive the CDs humidity sensor. Fig. 5d shows the current variation of the TENG-driven CDs humidity sensor under different RHs. A gradually increasing current is observed as the humidity increases from 11–94% RH (Fig. 5e), which coincides with the trend under external power supply. Furthermore, the current values of the humidity sensor almost remain constant for each RH level before and after one month (Fig. S3), confirming the long-term stability and durability. Meanwhile, the output voltage and current of the TENG exhibit no obvious difference within one month (Figs. S4 and S5). More importantly, the self-powered sensor system exhibits excellent stability with no more than a five percent drop in current under different humidity levels within one month (Fig. S6). All these long-term stability tests confirm the high-performance of the self-powered sensor system.

The reliable signals are recorded for nearly 100 s under three different respiratory patterns, namely, deep, normal and rapid breathing. The working process of the self-powered system for rapid breathing (Movie S1) and normal breathing (Movie S2) are shown in movie S1 and movie S2. As shown in Fig. 5f, the respiratory intensity and rate of different breath patterns are significantly different. Rapid breathing generates more groups of peaks than normal breathing within the same time (Fig. 5g and h). The respiration rate of normal breathing is about 18 breaths/min, while it can reach 54 breaths/min for rapid breathing. The system can also work in deep breathing mode (Movie S3). When breathing deeply, the current intensity becomes higher and the rate becomes lower (Fig. 5i). Therefore, one can easily distinguish the patterns of human respiration from the distinct frequency and intensity of the breathing signals. The developed TENG-CDs humidity sensor system can monitor human breathing in real time without external power sources, promising a low-cost, portable, and self-powered electronic device for clinical breathing monitoring.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.addma.2020.101681.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S1, Video S2, Video S3.
Video S1
Video S2
Video S3
.

In summary, CD-based humidity sensors have been developed, and the sensors have been employed to real-time respiratory monitoring. The humidity sensor exhibits a relatively high sensitivity of 5318% at 94% RH, and superior stability and repeatability over a wide RH range (11–94%) for one month. Such outstanding sensing capabilities can be attributed to the hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of the CDs, which is beneficial for absorbing water molecules. Furthermore, TENG-driven humidity sensors were further constructed to realize effectively the recognition of different human breathing modes. The results provide a strategy for CDs based humidity sensors, and prospects their applications in respiratory monitoring.

Jinxu Qin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xigui Yang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Chenglong Shen: Conceptualization, Supervision. Yu Chang: Methodology. Yuan Deng: Methodology. Zhenfeng Zhang: Resources. Hang Liu: Methodology, Resources. Chaofan Lv: Resources. Yizhe Li: Resources. Chuang Zhang: Resources. Lin Dong: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Chong-Xin Shan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 12174348, 52072345, 62027816, and 11974317), the Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by CAST (2021QNRC001), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Nos. 2018M630830 and 2019T120631), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (212300410410), and Henan Center for Outstanding Overseas Scientists (GZS201903).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nanoen.2022.107549.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Single-electrode-based sliding triboelectric nanogenerator for self-powered displacement vector sensor system

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yang, H. Zhang, Q. Jing, Y.S. Zhou, X. Wen, Z.L. Wang, Single-electrode-based sliding triboelectric nanogenerator for self-powered displacement vector sensor system, ACS Nano 7 (2013) 7342–7351.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jinxu Qin received his Bachelor’s degree in Northeastern University in 2018. Now he is pursuing his doctor’s degree in Zhengzhou University. His research interest focuses on the synthesis of nanoparticles and their optoelectronic application.

Asso. Prof. Xigui Yang received his Ph.D degree (2017) in State Key Laboratory of Superhard Materials from Jilin University and then joined Zhengzhou University in 2017. He is currently an associate professor in School of Physics & Microelectronics, Zhengzhou University. His research interest is mainly focused on diamond based optoelectronic devices and the structure-property relationships of functional materials under high pressure.

Dr. Chenglong Shen is an assistant research fellow at School of Physics and Microelectronics, Zhengzhou University. He received his B.S. degree (2017) and Ph.D. degree (2021) from Zhengzhou University. Since 2021, he entered into Zhengzhou University as a research fellow in the Diamond Optoelectronic Materials and Devices Group directed by Prof. Chong-Xin Shan. His present research interests are carbon-related materials and their applications.

Yu Chang received her Bachelor’s degree in Xinzhou Normal University in 2019. She is currently a postgraduate student in the School of Physics & Microelectronics, Zhengzhou University. Her research focuses on flexible multifunctional electroluminescence devices.
Yuan Deng received her Bachelor’s degree in ShaoYang University in 2018. She is pursuing her doctor’s degree in Zhengzhou University. Her research focuses on stress luminescent material.

Zhenfeng Zhang received his Bachelor’s degree in Henan University of Technology in 2018. Now he is pursuing his doctor’s degree in Zhengzhou University. His research interest focuses on the synthesis of diamonds and their optoelectronic application.

Hang Liu received his Bachelor’s degree in Henan University of Science and Technology in 2021. He is currently a postgraduate student in the School of Physics & Microelectronics, Zhengzhou University. His research focuses on wide bandgap semiconductor materials.

Chaofan Lv received his Bachelor’s degree in Xinyang Normal University in 2017. Now he is pursuing his doctor’s degree in Zhengzhou University. His research interest focuses on the nitrogen-vacancy center.

Yizhe Li received her Bachelor’s degree in Zhongyuan University of Technology in 2019. She is currently a postgraduate student in the School of Physics & Microelectronics, Zhengzhou University. Her research focuses on two-dimension nano-materials.

Chuang Zhang received his Bachelor’s degree in North China University of Water Resources and Electric Power in 2020. He is currently a postgraduate student in the School of Physics & Microelectronics, Zhengzhou University. His research focuses on ultrahard material.

Prof. Lin Dong received his B.S. (1998) and M.S. degree (2001) in Chemistry from Jilin University, and the PhD degree (2005) in Condensed Matter Physics from Changchun Institute of Optics, Fine Mechanics and Physics, CAS. He joined Zhengzhou University in 2005. He is currently a professor in School of Physics & Microelectronics, Zhengzhou University. His research interest is mainly focused on mechanoluminescence and piezotronic/piezophototronic derived nanomaterials and devices.

Prof. Chong-Xin Shan obtained his BS degree from Wuhan University in 1999, and PhD degree from Changchun Institute of Optics, Fine Mechanics and Physics (CIOMP), CAS in 2004. Then he worked in sequence in Chinese University of Hong Kong and University of Nottingham as a postdoctoral researcher, and returned to CIOMP as “Hundred Talent” professor in 2008. He moved to Zhengzhou University in 2015, and is currently the Dean of in School of Physics & Microelectronics. His research interest is mainly focused on wide band gap semiconductor materials and optoelectronic devices.
